data.title: January Dogs Of The Dow: One Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Buy
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Dow Industrials is too pricey and reveals only skinny dividends, January (again) finds only Verizon (VZ), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price and it is also ‘safer’ because it has more-than-enough free-cash-flow to support its dividends.

In late February 2024 Dow Jones replaced the low priced high yield dividend payer Walgreens, with Amazon (AMZN), a high-priced non-dividend payer. More recently, in November 2024, Intel (INTC) was replaced by Nvidia (NVDA) and Dow Inc (DOW) was supplanted by Sherwin-Williams (SHW).

With renewed downside market pressure up to 72.52% all ten of the top ‘safer’ January Dow dividend dogs could become elite fair-priced buys with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced Dow Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 17.86% To 33.07% Net Gains From Top-Ten Dow Dogs By January 2027

Four of ten top dividend-yielding Dow dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this January, 2027 yield-based forecast for Dow dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding Dow stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2026-27 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to January, 2027 were:

NVIDIA (NVDA) was projected to net $330.70, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 64 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 132% greater than the market as a whole.

Microsoft (MSFT) was projected to net $285.12, based on the median of target price estimates from 58 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 8% greater than the market as a whole.

Verizon (VZ) was projected to net $247.41, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% Under the market as a whole.

Procter & Gamble (PG) was projected to net $238.39 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 61% under to the market as a whole.

Salesforce (CRM) was projected to net $237.05, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 58 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% over the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $233.91, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 39 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% over the market as a whole.

Amazon (AMZN) was projected to net $213.30, based on the median of target price estimates from 67 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 37% more than the market as a whole.

Coca-Cola (KO) was projected to net $198.76, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 61% less than the market as a whole.

Honeywell (HON) was projected to net $191.74 based on the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% under the market as a whole.

Disney (DIS) was projected to net $178.56, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 31 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 44% above the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 23.55% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Dow Index stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 8% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

30 Dow Dogs By 1Yr Total Returns

30 Dow Dogs By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The January, 2026 Dow 30 By Yield

No January, 2026 Dow 30 Dogs Made the Top Ten in All Three Ranks: Returns, Targets, & Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top January Dow Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.51% To 6.88%

Top ten Dow Dogs represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The lone communication services dog placed first, Verizon [1], to lead the pack. Then, in second place was another loner; this one from the energy sector, Chevron [2].

Four healthcare stocks placed third, sixth, ninth, and tenth: Merck (MRK) [3], Amgen (AMGN) [6], United Health (UNH) [9], then Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) [10].

Two January consumer staple stalwarts, placed fourth and fifth, Procter& Gamble (PG) [4], and Coca Cola Co (KO) [5].

Finally, two consumer discretionary members placed seventh and eighth, Home Depot (HD) [7], and Nike (NKE) [8], to complete the January top-ten Dogs of the Dow by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten January Dow dogs by dividend and price as of market close 1/7/2026.

Nearly all top-ten Dow dogs show a less than ideal status because the prices of nine exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just one of the five lowest priced Dogs of the Dow. Only Verizon, lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Dow Index Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 1/7/26

The above chart reveals thirty Dow dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

Two stocks in the Dow index pay no dividend and are, therefore, excluded from the calculation. For those that count, December’s least expensive dog of the Dow is Verizon $14.54 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVIDIA (NVDA) at $4,716.98 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): One of Twenty-Eight Dow Dividend Dogs Show No or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

No or a negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividend, The January pair are Coca-Cola, and 3M. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields equaling or exceeding free cash flow yields.

Two cut their dividends in the 2020 Ides of March depression times, Boeing, and The Walt Disney Co. One more Salesforce (CRM), snuck onto the Dow index without a dividend.

However, in March 2023, Salesforce declared its first-ever quarterly payout and Disney, in January, 2024 rejoined the dividend paying ranks. However four (two short on cash and two non-dividend payers, Amazon, and Boeing) cast a pall on the Dow by being stockholder adverse.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, TACO deals, wars, ICE, and vigilante actions, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Rerates Could Get All Ten Dow Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, one in the top ten is already ideally priced. Beside Verizon, already in the ideal zone, nine more top-yield stocks are within $253.15 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split shares. Those options may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 17.53% To 34.05% Top Ten Dow Index Upsides To January, 2027; (32), Three -2.41 to -6.00% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 34.12% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Dow Dogs To January, 2027

Ten-top Dow January updated yield (dividend / price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

Dow top ten noted 1/7/26 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. (Real Estate is not reported by the Dow and Utilities has its own Dow Index. Materials, Financials, Industrials, and Technology also missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Dow Dogs (34) To Deliver 19.27% Vs. (35) 14.37% Net Gains by All Ten Come January, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dow Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 34.12% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The very lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Verizon, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 24.74%.

The five lowest-priced Dow top-yield dogs for January were: Verizon; Nike; Cola-Cola; Merck; Procter & Gamble, with prices ranging from $40.13 to $138.04.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of January 7 were: Chevron; Johnson & Johnson; Amgen; UnitedHealth; Home Depot, with prices ranging from $155.20 to $349.06.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and Trade war-fueled slide gains momentum into 2026, with the exception of a very few Techno, and (maybe) Energy stocks, look for at least half of the ten Dow Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy One ‘Safer' Dow January DiviDog and Watch Four More

Early January 2026, 19 of the 30 Dow Index were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 1/7/26 data showed one was IDEAL and fair-priced.

How Dow 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Dow dogs conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. That one is Verizon Communications still standing after passing the ‘safer’ dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of 84.4% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the Dow updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations. Just remember Verizon In December 2025 and the five stand-by Dow dogs of the month: Cisco, Chevron, Procter & Gamble, Honeywell, and McDonald’s.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Countdown To March: Jobs Report Locks In Rate Cut
data.description:
Friday’s jobs report for December was a double-edged sword in that it was extremely market friendly but not indicative of a strengthening economy. Investors liked it because the lack of job growth should move the Fed to cut rates at its March meeting by another 25 basis points, which further eases financial conditions and supports risk asset prices. Economists didn’t like it because it indicates that businesses are still leery of the economic outlook, due to tariffs, trade policy, and concerns about consumer resilience.

According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the economy added 50,000 jobs last month, led by bars and restaurants, health care, and social assistance companies. Unfortunately, the prior two months were revised lower by 76,000, resulting in a net loss of 26,000 jobs. It is no coincidence that job growth fell off a cliff after April, following the rollout of President Trump’s trade policy. Tariffs are paid by businesses, which crimp profit margins and force owners to cut costs and limit expansion plans. The largest input cost for a business is labor. Perhaps tariffs will lead to an increase in business activity in the manufacturing sector that creates new jobs, but that hasn’t happened yet. We are paying the price upfront today for a payoff that some expect to come tomorrow. The ISM Manufacturing Index indicates that we have yet to see benefits, as it has been in contraction for ten consecutive months.

The loss of 268,000 federal workers clearly skewed the above results, but when we focus on private sector payrolls, the numbers are not that different. The private sector added 37,000 jobs last month, while the 70,000 in downward revisions to the prior two months left us with a loss of 33,000 jobs. This caps off the worst non-recession year for job creation since 2003. Yet we are likely to see a new normal, due to immigration policy, that requires fewer jobs each month to absorb the increase in labor supply. That number used to be approximately 100,000 per month, but it is now probably closer to 50,000.

On the wage front, average hourly earnings increased 0.3% in December and 3.8% over the past year. While the rate of growth has declined, which has been a goal of the Fed’s to bring down the rate of inflation, it is still holding above the rate of inflation. Real income growth is critical to continue realizing real consumer spending growth.

The unemployment rate edged lower from 4.5% to 4.4%, but that was largely due to a decline in the participation rate from 62.5% to 62.4%. Participation is likely to continue declining, given immigration policy, which will keep a lid on the unemployment rate. A lower unemployment rate looks better, but the primary driver of our labor force growth has been immigration. Labor force growth is an important source of economic growth. Slower labor force growth will lead to a slower rate of economic growth over time.

Therefore, the no-hire and no-fire labor market is still with us. If I have one concern, it is that the downward revisions in the number of jobs created are being accompanied by a decline in the length of the workweek. Usually, when job growth slows, we see an increase in the length of the workweek. Employers have fewer workers who work longer hours to make up the difference, but that is not the case today. The labor market is probably weaker than the headline numbers suggest, and that is why I think the Fed will act much sooner than the market expects with another rate cut. Following the jobs report, the probability of a rate cut at the March meeting was just 27%. I think over the coming weeks that will increase to a near guarantee, as the effective tariff rate is lowered by the Supreme Court, disinflation resumes, and the labor market remains soft.

The good news is that our frozen labor market has not adversely affected the rate of consumer spending growth, which is the primary engine of our economy. I think we can thank a raging bull market for that, as the wealth effect is powering spending for the top 50% of households, with a particular emphasis on the top 10%. This K-shaped economic expansion is not a problem for the market today, but when we approach that latest stage of this business cycle, it will be a key factor in how deep the inevitable drawdown is in the next bear market. I don’t see this as a factor in 2026.

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 1
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead.

AbbVie Inc.

By Tom Hutchinson, Editor, Cabot Dividend Investor

AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) is a US-based biopharmaceutical company formed in 2013 as a spinoff from Abbott Laboratories (ABT). It’s a cutting-edge company with strong exposure to high-demand needs in immunology and oncology... and it has a terrific pipeline.

AbbVie became an industry giant because of its mega-blockbuster drug Humira. It’s an autoimmune medication that became the world’s bestselling drug with annual sales of over $20 billion. But the tremendous success of that drug became a problem as Humira lost its patent overseas a few years ago, and it lost its US patent in 2023.

Because of shrinking Humira sales, AbbVie posted lower year-over-year revenues until late last year. But the company turned that corner. AbbVie has long planned for this eventuality and has done a stellar job launching new drugs capable of replacing the diminishing Humira revenue.

Humira accounted for 75% of revenue a few years ago. But new immunology drugs Skyrizi and Rinvoq together now have sales that already replace peak Humira revenues. In the most recent quarter, the two drugs had combined revenue of $6.9 billion, on pace to substantially outsell the best Humira year.

AbbVie has also guided for the two drugs to bring in $31 billion in 2027 and $40 billion by 2029. While those drugs are killing it, AbbVie also has a robust pipeline of new drugs in the hopper, including important cutting-edge indications in the areas of blood cancer and Parkinson’s. In fact, there are currently 20 drugs in phase III, the final phase before approval. AbbVie also currently has over 50 drugs in earlier development phases.

Despite the market-beating performance of the past several years, the stock was held back by the patent issues and falling revenue. Having turned the corner, it looks like better days have already arrived. ABBV was up more than 20% in 2025.

Don’t forget the dividend. The company is paying out $6.92 per year, which translated to a 3.1% yield at the recent price. AbbVie has also grown the dividend by an average of 12.5% per year over the last 10 years and raised the payout annually... including when it was part of Abbott Labs... for 53 years.

Recommended Action: Buy ABBV.

Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc.

By Tim Melvin, Editor of The Flagship Report

Investors rarely get a chance to buy a dominant franchise at a price that assumes the business is headed for long-term trouble. That is exactly the situation with Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (ARE) moving into 2026.

The market has lumped Alexandria together with generic office REITs, even though its assets are not ordinary office towers. These are mission-critical life science campuses anchored by the best credit tenants in biotechnology, research, pharmaceuticals, and elite universities. The replacement cost of these properties is soaring, while the demand for true lab-capable space continues to grow.

When we worked through the fundamentals, the numbers pointed to a company that has already endured its valley and is positioned for a powerful recovery cycle. Leasing spreads have stabilized, and occupancy has proven far more resilient than the “office contagion” narrative would suggest.

Meanwhile, development yields remain attractive because Alexandria continues to build in the highest-barrier-to-entry markets in the country. And the balance sheet has been deliberately strengthened through selective asset sales, laddered debt maturities, and an extended runway of fixed-rate obligations.

I used credit models to answer the question that every aggressive investor must ask about a leveraged real estate company: What is the probability that equity is impaired under stressed conditions? The models showed that Alexandria’s implied distance-to-default remains solidly in the healthy zone. Even under conservative assumptions and wider credit spreads, the probability of distress was low.

The equity behaves like a temporarily out-of-favor call option on a trophy-asset landlord whose underlying collateral value continues to rise. For an aggressive investor, that is exactly the structure you want. The recent dividend cut should reinforce the already-strong balance sheet further.

The story for 2026 is about normalization and re-rating. Capital markets are healing. Life science R&D funding is improving. Venture flows are slowly reviving. Tenants are re-activating leasing decisions that were deferred during the past two years of macro uncertainty.

At the same time, its campuses sit at the center of the innovation economy - a long-duration secular trend that remains firmly intact. The gap between Alexandria’s share price and the intrinsic value of its real estate is wide enough to offer aggressive investors a compelling opportunity.

I believe 2026 will mark the beginning of that gap closing. Even after the recent dividend cut, we will get paid to wait for the recovery to take hold, as the shares recently yielded more than 5%.

Recommended Action: Buy ARE.

Arbor Realty Trust Inc.

By Tim Plaehn, Editor of The Dividend Hunter

For over a decade, Arbor Realty Trust Inc. (ABR) was a standout dividend growth finance REIT. From 2012 through the first quarter of 2025, the ABR dividend grew by 300%. It faced some issues in 2025, but I expect ABR to be the high-yield turnaround story of 2026.

The Federal Reserve’s rapid increase in interest rates, starting in 2022 at near-zero percent and peaking at over 5% in 2023, was a severe jolt to Arbor Realty’s business and to investors in ABR shares. Arbor Realty primarily provides financing for multi-family property investors. Loans on these types of property investments almost always have adjustable-rate interest rates.

As the Fed kept rates high for several years, the stress on the Arbor loans grew, forcing them to make modifications or even take back properties - real estate-owned (REO). A large number of loans were placed in delinquent status.

After a long history of dividend growth with no reductions, the company had to slash the payout by 30% in May 2025. The current $0.30 dividend has been paid for three straight quarters. But now that interest rates are declining, Arbor Realty can be viewed as having two distinct business operations.

The current business lines, which include packaging loans for agency mortgage-backed securities, a bridge lending program, a single-family rentals lending program, and construction lending, are performing very well. The other side of the business is what Arbor management refers to as the legacy loan book, many of which are in trouble.

For the third quarter, the company reported delinquent assets of $750 million, up from $529 million at the end of the second quarter. The REO book was at $470 million at the end of the third quarter. This one billion-plus of assets are not earning interest, and thus the source of the dividend cut a few quarters ago.

But the Arbor management team is accelerating the classification of loans as delinquent and the recovery of properties. Working through delinquent loans and REO will free up capital that is currently earning nothing. That capital will be added to equity to put to work in current business operations.

They also expect to continue paying the current ($0.30) dividend rate. ABR now yields over 13%. So, if you buy shares now, you’ll receive an excellent yield, and there is a significant chance of substantial share price appreciation in 2026.

Recommended Action: Buy ABR.

Archer Aviation

By Carl Delfeld, Editor of Cabot Explorer

Traffic jams in urban centers always prompt thoughts of the Jetsons and their flying cars whisking them to work. Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) has emerged as one of the most intriguing ways to play this theme. The company’s strategy is to be a leader for short-haul transportation in metro areas and for military and defense purposes.

Improvements in electric batteries and vertical takeoff technology are bringing this science fiction dream to reality. Morgan Stanley projects that what they call “urban air vertical takeoff and landing” (eVTOL), like an electric helicopter, will be between a $1.5 trillion and $2.9 trillion market by 2040.

ACHR stock has surged, then pulled back, as the company races toward Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) certification. Archer is an aggressive play, since it will post no revenue this year. But it has signaled its seriousness, having just bought an airport in Los Angeles that will serve as a flagship for its LA area air taxi network.

The acquisition of Hawthorne Airport was for $126 million in cash. Hawthorne is about three miles from LAX (Los Angeles International Airport), and the company has been named as the exclusive air taxi provider for the 2028 Summer Olympics. The LA metropolis provides Archer with the right geography and economic base.

The Hawthorne acquisition also provides Archer with a dedicated platform for its AI-powered operations as it codevelops with Palantir Technologies Inc.(PLTR), the US Air Force, and Anduril on defense programs.

Archer’s Midnight aircraft is a piloted electric takeoff vehicle that can house four passengers. In a recent test flight, a pilot flew 55 miles and averaged 126 miles per hour, all with a powerful electric motor for takeoff and landing. Instead of one propeller, the vehicle has 12, so if one fails, the aircraft and pilot can continue to fly.

Archer reported total operating expenses of $174.8 million in Q3, up from $122.1 million in the quarter a year ago, and its net loss was $129.9 million. Archer finished the third quarter with $1.65 billion in cash and short-term investments - and raised an additional $650 million, giving it roughly $2.3 billion in cash.

Archer has received more than a billion dollars’ worth of orders for its Midnight aircraft, but it’s unclear when those will be fulfilled. The same could be said for this speculative and exciting stock. FAA certification would be an important milestone.

Recommended Action: Buy ACHR.

Ares Management Corp.

By Adrian Day, Editor of Global Analyst

It has been a bumpy few months for Business Development Companies (BDCs) - companies that lend money to small private businesses which are passed on to investors as high dividend yields. But I like one of the strongest, as well as one of the oldest and largest, Ares Management Corp. (ARCC).

The concerns are three-fold. First, as the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, BDC returns will be reduced, given that most BDC loans have floating rates. Second, there is concern about how small businesses will hold up in a weakening economy. Third, and arguably most worrying, are concerns about private credit, following a couple of spectacular credit failures in a market that has experienced tremendous growth in recent years.

Though these concerns are all valid, there are some misunderstandings. Further, the strongest of the BDCs will overcome the problems. Let’s look at the concerns one by one.

Lower interest rates will reduce income, other things being equal. But since many BDCs have floating-rate debt as well, what they lose on one side of the ledger, they gain on the other. And in lending, the spread is most important.

Although a slower economy will make things more difficult for some small businesses, lower rates are likely to help companies in their portfolios. Moreover, in periods of economic weakness, banks pull in their reins. That leaves the strongest small business to go to alternate lenders, such as BDCs.

Private credit is an area of concern, primarily because of how the lenders are valuing their loans. Often one can find wildly different marks from different lenders on the very same loan (in a large syndicated loan, for instance).

However, the better BDCs use independent valuation firms. In the case of Ares, it uses four different valuers on a rotating basis. So each loan is valued by four different firms at least once a year. This is the gold standard in the industry.

Ares’ portfolio has strong credit ratings, with non-accruals at 1.7% at cost, below the industry average as well as its own historical average. It is also very broadly diversified, with 587 businesses in 27 industries.

Ares has had only four quarterly losses over two decades, with fewer than 20 basis points of average annualized debt losses since inception in 2004. The track record, the diversification, its large spillover income cushion (equivalent to more than the full dividend for two quarters), and its low 1.02x leverage should enable Ares to maintain its dividend in all but the most extreme circumstances.

That dividend yield was recently more than 9%, making Ares a nice cornerstone in an income portfolio.

Recommended Action: Buy ARCC.

Aris Mining Corp.

By Sean Brodrick, Editor at Weiss Ratings Daily

Aris Mining Corp. (ARMN) may be based in Vancouver, but its future is being forged in Latin America - and that future is getting brighter. The company is rapidly emerging as one of the most dynamic and profitable gold producers in the region, with major expansions underway across its flagship assets in Colombia and Guyana.

If management delivers, Aris could evolve from a regional operator into a 500,000-ounce-per-year powerhouse by 2027. That’s transformational.

Aris is executing one of the most ambitious organic growth plans in the gold sector today. Management is guiding for 230,000 ounces to 275,000 ounces in 2025, and by 2027, it expects output to exceed 500,000 ounces annually - nearly doubling current levels.

Recent financials show the momentum is real: Q3 earnings surged to 36 cents a share, a scorching 350% rise from a year earlier. Margins are widening, free cash flow is building, and earnings are poised to run even higher. Wall Street sees a 225% jump in earnings in 2025, followed by another 80% gain in 2026.

Best of all, this expansion is fully funded. Aris ended Q3 2025 with $415 million in cash, and debt has plunged to just $64 million, down dramatically from $241 million at the end of 2024.

Aris Mining’s rise is anchored by four world-class projects that give it diversified, multi-jurisdictional strength:

Segovia (Colombia) - One of the highest-grade underground gold mines on the planet. A mill expansion is ramping production toward 300,000 ounces per year by 2026, powered by consistent high-grade feed and excellent cost control.

Marmato (Colombia) - A major underground buildout is underway, targeting first production in late 2026. This will become a second large, long-life complex feeding Aris’s growth engine.

Soto Norte (Colombia, JV with Mubadala) - A tier-one undeveloped deposit with a robust PFS. Aris owns 51% and operates it. The permitting progress could unlock massive upside.

Toroparu (Guyana) - A new PEA outlines 235,000 ounces per year over a 21-year mine life with strong returns. This represents Aris’s next-generation project pipeline and long-term value lever.

In this constructive gold price environment, mid-tier producers with expanding high-grade output are primed for outsize gains. Aris is perfectly timed for this cycle: costs are stable, cash flow is robust, and each project milestone adds real, material production growth without taking on risky leverage.

Aris Mining is a rapidly scaling, cash flow-positive producer with one of the clearest multi-asset growth stories in the sector. With production set to blow past 500,000 ounces by 2027, multiple expansions moving forward, and a fortress balance sheet, Aris is positioning itself as Latin America’s next mid-tier champion.

AST SpaceMobile Inc.

By Chris Preston, Chief Analyst at Cabot Stock of the Week

AST SpaceMobile Inc. (ASTS) has the very ambitious goal of delivering broadband internet service via low-Earth-orbit satellites to... every smartphone in the world. Quite the undertaking! And yet, with backing from some of the biggest names in telecommunications, Wall Street thinks it can get there (or at least close).

The stock has more than tripled in the last 18 months. But there are reasons to believe its run is just getting started. In September 2024, AST launched the first six of its so-called BlueBird satellites. This past December, it launched a more advanced version in India called the BlueBird 6 - which is 3.5 times larger than the previous BlueBird 1-5 launches and supports 10 times the data capacity.

It would be the largest commercial satellite array in low-Earth orbit at 2,400 square feet. It’s just the beginning of AST’s ambitious upcoming launch phase, as AST plans another five orbital launches in the first quarter of 2026, with 40 total set for this year. That bodes well for the stock price over the next 12 months.

The accompanying investor buzz that has preceded AST’s previous satellite launches has resulted in huge gaps up in the share price, including a better-than-50% boost in the first half of December, prior to the Dec. 15 BlueBird 6 launch.

Big-name partnerships have expedited AST’s launch schedule. AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), STC Group, British telecom giant Vodafone (VOD), and the US government have all inked deals with AST to help it build out its globe-spanning network - and deliver $1 billion in contracted revenue. All told, the company has agreements with more than 50 mobile operators, with a reach of three billion subscribers around the world.

There are risks here. The stock is notoriously volatile, plummeting from $95 to $50 in just over a month after quarterly results (a $122.9 million net loss) disappointed. And the valuation is sky-high.

But so is the upside. The overall trajectory is decidedly up, despite all the wild gyrations. And the business is just getting started, with more satellite launches planned this year than in its entire previous history. If the bull market marches on in 2026, as I think it will, AST should continue to skyrocket. Pun intended.

Recommended Action: Buy ASTS.

Originally posted on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Data Center Power And Evolving Car Architectures Support Upside For Renesas Electronics
data.description:
Semiconductor stocks have come back to life, with evidence of leaner inventories and demand recoveries outside of the much-vaunted and much-valued data center market. While I don’t expect demand to come roaring back in 2026, I think the stage is set for an early cyclical upturn, and I expect Japan’s Renesas Electronics (RNECF) (RNECY) (6723.T) to benefit.

Renesas has had so-so performance since my last update, with investors less than impressed by management reducing mid-term margin targets due to increased R&D investments. While a 40% move isn’t bad, peers and rivals like Microchip (MCHP) and Infineon (IFNNY) have done substantially better and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) and STMicro (STM) have more or less matched Renesas’s performance.

While I do have concerns that elevated R&D spending could cap (weigh on) upside for the shares, I do like the company’s leverage to growth opportunities in autos (ADAS and software-defined architectures) and data center (GaN power for 800V), as well as longer-term recovery opportunities in industrial automation/electrification and IoT. Given where the valuation is today, I see a case for owning the shares.

Easy Comps Should Drive A Return To Year-Over-Year Growth

Renesas has logged three quarters in a row of modest sequential growth, fueled in large part by improvements in the company’s core auto control and newer ADAS offerings, but also helped by growing success in the data center market (memory interfaces and power management (or PMICs)).

The coming fourth quarter report should see a significant spike in the year-over-year comp, helped by ongoing growth in those key drivers, but also helped by an easy year-ago comp. With all of that, I expect around 18% yoy growth and I think the odds are that the company will come in on the high side (or above) of the current sell-side range.

I likewise see upside on margins. Renesas has done a better job of managing this downturn than it has been credited for, and while management guides have been cautious, the company has been beating operating income estimates by about 5%, and I think there is some upside here.

Easier Comps, Market Recoveries, And New Wins Support A Reacceleration In 2026 And Beyond

To be clear from the outset, I’m not expecting a V-shaped recovery in the broader semiconductor market in 2026. There’s still a lot of caution out there in major markets like industrial and consumer, and I expect customers and distributors to be cautious about rebuilding inventory at least in the first half of the year. I also expect another lackluster year for underlying auto production, particularly with demand slowing in China.

Even against that so-so backdrop, I see a path for high-single digit, and possibly double-digit, revenue growth for Renesas through content growth in autos and data centers and the benefits of recent wins in mobile that will ramp in 2026.

Autos – Maybe Not Vroom-Vroom, But Out Of Neutral

The auto market remains a critical one for Renesas, contributing close to half of revenue, and most analysts are looking for flat to low single-digit growth in 2026, with most of the growth in emerging markets. North America and Europe could well be down slightly (even though affordability has been improving), and there’s a real risk of slight contraction in China, as well as lackluster demand in China.

That’s not a great environment for Renesas, but I expect content growth to more than compensate for underlying volume challenges. The company is ramping up its 28nm MCUs for auto customers and these launches are helping the company regain share in the auto market (where it is second behind Infineon and ahead of NXP Semiconductors).

It will take time for this play out fully, as design lives are usually measured in years (and OEMs won’t typically switch in the middle), but in the near term the company is benefitting from customers switching to the newest offerings. Renesas is also benefitting from platform wins from Chinese OEMs, and as local brands continue to gain share in the market, this is valuable volume driver (even in a slowing market).

Renesas is also benefiting from content growth tied to changes in auto designs. Cars are shipping with increasingly sophisticated and capable ADAS, and that drives content growth for Renesas (more sophisticated systems require more chips and more advanced chips), particularly its R-Car Gen 4 family.

The company is also on the front end of a significant shift in how cars are designed and built. OEMs are increasingly shifting toward common architectures were features are defined and controlled by software, meaning that features like keyless entry or remote start may be shipped on all cars, but only activated if the buyer chooses that option (or subscribes/buys the option later). MCUs are vital components in these more complicated systems and as more of the car becomes electrified and software-controlled (not just the powertrain), it will drive more demand for Renesas MCUs, PMICs, and other chip types.

Data Center Is Starting To Add Up

Renesas is not new to the data center, but the company is definitely seeing this business go to a new level where it is a material contributor to growth. Around half of this business is in memory interfaces, and the DDR5 cycle is driving demand growth for these chips on top of market share growth.

The more interesting opportunity to me is in power chips. Much has been written about how power-hungry data centers are, but that power has to be controlled and directed within the systems, and that’s where Renesas’s PMICs are seeing increasing uptake. Not surprisingly given their position in memory interfaces, Renesas is strong in memory module power, but also in applications like rack power systems, switches, and storage/SSD controllers.

Two developments in particular have my attention. First, the company has unveiled a GaN solution for 800V DC architecture (supporting Nvidia’s (NVDA) architecture), and this will enable 800V direct current buses within racks – reducing power distribution losses and the need for large bus bars. Second, the company will be ramping power devices for custom ASICs in 2026, and custom silicon has been a huge growth driver for Broadcom (AVGO), as well as Marvell (MRVL) to some extent, and custom ASICs are seeing growing uptake in hyperscale data centers.

Bits And Pieces To Also Consider

One of the bigger shifts since my last update on Renesas is the company’s decision to suspend development of SiC and IGBT power devices, primarily for the auto market (but also industrial to some extent).

This decision was motivated in part by Wolfspeed’s (WOLF) struggles in a glutted SiC market (the two companies had a sizable supply agreement), as well as shifts in the market such that the company no longer believes it needs to offer these power devices to maintain/gain share with its MCUs and other auto-focused products.

This is not a permanent exit from the market, and in the meantime the company will redirect its R&D resources toward GaN power. It’s also worth noting that, by the terms of the prior agreement with Wolfspeed, Renesas could end up owning about a third of the company (pending regulatory approval, including CFIUS), giving the company rather direct exposure to a potential recovery in SiC demand (and Wolfspeed itself).

The company is now also reportedly pursuing a sale of its timing business, with reports claiming that a major investment bank has been engaged and potential buyers like Texas Instruments (TXN), Infineon, and SiTime have at least kicked the tires. Multiple reports have suggested a $2B price tag, and that’s a strong offer for a business that I’d estimate is generating around $350M-$400M in revenue. While not a bad business by any means, it doesn’t really fit with where Renesas is focusing its R&D and product efforts.

Those R&D efforts bring me to my last point. Management announced at its midyear Capital Markets Day that it would be ramping up its R&D efforts, with a focus on digitalization and software development. Management claimed that recent geopolitical turbulence was a “wake up call” that it needs to focus on more than just hardware, and it wants digitalization and UX to be at least 10% of revenue in the future. Companies like Broadcom, Infineon, NXP, and STMicro have done well on the basis of strong internal software capabilities, and I think this is a logical decision for Renesas given the direction that auto and industrial automation and electrification are going.

The impact, though, is going to be higher R&D spending and weaker operating margins. Management reduced its operating margin target from 30% to 25% to 30% and pushed its margin goals for its “Aspiration 2030” to 2035. While management has a reputation for conservative guidance, margins are significant in driving semiconductor multiples, so this will definitely be a closely-watched (and likely criticized) decision as it plays out in the coming years.

The Outlook

My estimates for revenue for Renesas in 2025 and 2026 are only slightly lower than before, mostly due to a slower pace of recovery in industrial markets. I expect significant reacceleration in the business, though, with three-year growth of around 6% and five-year growth of around 9% on strength in autos (system electrification and software-defined architectures), data center (memory interfaces and power), and industrial (automation/electrification), as well as ongoing opportunities in IoT and consumer devices. Longer term, I expect revenue growth of around 6%.

On the margin side, I do have concerns that heavier R&D spending will compress the margin recovery in this upcoming up-cycle. Management may be taking a conservative approach with guidance, but I’m not looking for a recovery to prior highs over the next five years. I do think margins will be sufficient to support free cash flow margins in the low-to-mid-20%’s (helped by reduced capex spending), though, and that should drive double-digit FCF growth from a low 2025 starting point.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue, I believe Renesas is around 20% to 30% undervalued today, with a 4.65x multiple in my FY’26 revenue estimate supporting the higher end of that range.

The Bottom Line

The risk of lackluster margin leverage in this next phase of the cycle is definitely a potential headwind for the shares, but I believe increased sales and share of higher-value products like 28nm MCUs and GaN power devices will drive some margin momentum, as will improvements in utilization (which currently sits around 50%). With underrated leverage to new products and key end-market recoveries , as well as emerging leverage to data center capex, I think Renesas is still worth owning here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capital Southwest: An All-Weather BDC For Retirees, Yielding 10%
data.description:
In the past couple of days, the BDC land has been extremely volatile, registering significant ups and downs. But still, on a YTD basis, the sector has managed to advance, generally in line with the overall stock market.

It seems that 2026 could be quite a rollercoaster ride for the equity market and, of course, BDCs, which have been under pressure due to rising interest rates and increased fears of what Jamie Dimon characterized as cockroaches - i.e., the emergence of non-accruals in the form of struggling private credit companies.

Yet, as most of my closest followers know, I am extremely bullish on BDCs - both in the short and long term.

When it comes to the short-term part of the equation, we can take advantage of the discounted valuations and attractive BDC total yield spreads relative to alternative yield-bearing securities (e.g., benchmark U.S. 10-year) even after considering some form of SOFR-driven dividend cuts. For example, just a couple of days ago, CION Investment Corporation (CION) announced a dividend cut, after which the yield dropped from 15.8% to 12.4%. In my view, this kind of current income level could be deemed fairly attractive.

When it comes to the long-term part of the equation, by deploying capital into BDCs, we can extract the benefit of a structural shift from regulated banking space to a more flexible private credit area, where the private credit component comes with robust underwriting, active management, and an inherent tilt to the most secured area in the company capital structures - i.e., senior secured first lien. In a nutshell, I view BDCs as above average yield-bearing assets that come with below-average risk that is typically associated with comparable yield levels.

With this being said, let me dissect Capital Southwest (CSWC), which, in my humble opinion, delivers on both fronts. It embodies the necessary fundamentals to produce defensive dividends and stable returns in the near term (as we ride the volatility and interest rate wave). Also, due to CSWC's unique positioning, it is well-positioned for long-term value creation - and here I am talking about economic returns, which is sustainable dividends and slight NAV appreciation.

Let me flesh out my thought process here.

Thesis

I would like to start with CSWC's yield, which currently stands at around 11.5%. This yield consists of two parts: a quarterly base dividend of $0.58 per share and a supplemental dividend of $0.06 per share. Even though I am very bullish on CSWC, I do not think that we should anchor our current income expectations around receiving the supplemental dividend going forward.

The reason is simple. CSWC has a notable chunk of its portfolio linked to floating-rate loan investments, which yield progressively less as the SOFR declines. As we know, the Fed has made three consecutive base rate cuts, which have not yet been reflected in BDC financials. While CSWC has an offsetting floating liability book, it is still insufficient to fully neutralize the SOFR-induced NII decline, especially considering the fact that CSWC is located in the bottom quartile in terms of capital structure (i.e., low debt to equity, at 1.09x). To put it differently, since equity is a fixed capital source, it does not provide the same offset as, say, a floating rate unsecured note.

However, on a base dividend basis, we are still getting a yield that is slightly higher than 10%, which I think is fairly attractive in the context of what I will articulate below.

I have three fundamental points to make about CSWC.

First, to finish the leverage story, we have to understand that having an overcapitalized balance sheet is a net positive. Sure, in the immediate term it acts as a headwind because of its fixed nature as opposed to a floating liability, but that is nicely balanced with the significant opportunity to expand the loan book right when the interest rate environment is favorable for taking such activity. In the Q3 BDC reports, we could see that M&A activity has returned to management agendas across the board. It also coincides with the bullish outlook for 2026, which is only logical given the reduced discount rates that tend to push valuations up and induce transaction activity. CSWC, having the necessary firepower (i.e., significant debt capacity without impairing the credit profile), should be able to take advantage of the trend.

Second, and this is a big one, CSWC is one of the equity-oriented BDCs in the field. What I mean by that is that, as of now, CSWC has 9.1% of its portfolio tied to sound and cash-flow-producing equity stakes, which it has opened while making loan investments into specific portfolio companies. Again, the fact that M&A is gaining momentum and interest rates have become more supportive for valuations puts CSWC in a favorable spot to not only benefit as a lender (capital provider), but also as a seller (monetizing equity stakes). Now, usually, when these equity stakes get monetized, BDCs tend to register significant gains, especially if the divestitures have taken place for healthy positions. And it is exactly this gain component that could surprise us to the upside in terms of either distributing sizeable supplementals or simply putting capital to work to boost the portfolio size even further. The bigger the portfolio, the more the volume factor can offset the price factor, which, as I articulated above, is under pressure.

Third, CSWC carries a quite unique and nuanced advantage, which comes in the form of being an internally managed BDC and enjoying a P/NAV that is close to 1.2x. The internally managed aspect puts the incentive fee issue off the table and, in the current M&A environment, comes in handy by enabling CSWC to tap into the secondary equity market space. Namely, as CSWC trades at a premium, it can issue new equity, in an accretive manner, to really complement the debt-driven portfolio growth. Sure, the incremental NII per share generation is lower from an "equity + debt" move than from a pure play debt move, but still, it is supportive to the NII per share growth given the accretive nature of share issuances.

The bottom line

In a nutshell, CSWC is an all-weather BDC that is rightly positioned to deliver sustainable returns across different market cycles and interest rate environments.

The bias towards high-quality equity stakes coupled with a conservative balance sheet and internal management is the trifecta for weathering SOFR-driven headwinds and increased credit risk in the system (currently, the former is clearly more relevant).

As I have put it several times before in my previous articles, the equity co-investments that BDCs make introduce a negatively correlated return factor to SOFR. Namely, the lower the SOFR, the higher the value of equity positions, which is a complete opposite to the loan investment dynamics that are positively correlated with SOFR changes (i.e., lower SOFR, lower loan yields). The key is to have high-quality equity and at a sufficient scale to move the needle - both of which CSWC has.

data.title: DRLL: Worst House On A Bad Street
data.description:
Introduction

Energy-related ETFs have not performed well over the last year. This probably comes as no great surprise, given the near 30% decline in oil prices. The subject of this Strive Energy U.S. ETF, (DRLL) shows every indication of being the worst house on a bad street. It is the worst performing ETF in this sector, a trend that hasn't varied since its inception several years ago. Some of this underperformance may be due to the fact that DRLL carries a much higher expense ratio in comparison to similarly structured funds.

It is worth noting the sector itself has also seriously underperformed the SP 500 ETF (SPY) but substantially outperformed the U.S. Oil Fund (USO), which tracks futures in the underlying commodity.

DRLL was initiated to encourage companies to put excellence ahead of other "stakeholder" type considerations. Its co-founder, Vivek Ramaswamy, is an outspoken and enthusiastic capitalist and has actively used his platform as a business leader, candidate for President, and now candidate for the governorship of Ohio to argue against ESG/DEI and other non-shareholder friendly considerations.

One drag on DRLL's market appeal may actually be related to Mr. Ramaswamy's unabashed conservative political leanings and anti-ESG stance on corporate governance. Despite the anti-ESG fanfare with which Ramaswamy launched the fund before beginning his political career, current management under new CEO, Mathew Cole has deemphasized this strident language, suggesting that Strive needed to "recalibrate its woke meter." It also has deleted proxy voting guidelines from its current prospectus. The actual distinction between old DRLL and new DRLL may be a bit qualitative, as the company still has some very assertive, activist language on its website-

Proactively engaging with management teams and boards, Strive aims to unlock value in the U.S. energy sector by mandating companies to focus on excellence.

The thesis for owning DRLL

President Trump has strongly refocused American energy policy toward the production of domestic oil and gas. He views this as being imperative for our national energy security and for export revenue to other countries that lack our resources. There are some disconnects in my view between this goal and his relentless determination to drive down energy prices, but so far production has remained high and prices have declined, so chalk up a win for the administration and, of course, for drivers of Ford Super Duty F-150s.

DRLL's top ten holdings are all well-known American companies, including sector giants like Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). To be considered for inclusion in DRLL a stock must be a member of the Bloomberg U.S. 1500 index.

Below, the company puts forward their investment thesis for the American energy sector, noting strong tailwinds for the sector, and a commitment to using their voice to drive excellence and cost efficiency to create value. It sounds good. From my time in the industry, I can tell you there is already a relentless focus on excellence, cost efficiency, and value creation. But one more voice can't hurt. I guess.

This list reads like a "Who's Who" of the American energy cohort, although many of these companies are beginning to have an international footprint as their U.S.-based assets start to age out. An example would be EOG Resources (EOG) operations in Trinidad and Tobago.

A potential catalyst for DRLL

Unless you've been on Mars the last few days, news of the American removal of Venezuela's dictator Maduro and the subsequent takeover of the country's oil assets by the U.S. has reached your ears. By all reports, and it's entirely logical, the country's oil fields and physical assets are in a state of extreme disrepair and neglect. Mismanagement by the country's leadership for the past 2.5 decades has resulted in most leading Western oil companies and service suppliers withdrawing. Chevron is the only company remaining on the ground and producing and exporting Venezuela's crude.

Estimates for the country's ability to produce oil amount to some 3.5 mm BOPD out of a reserve base of over 300 billion bbls of proved reserves. Today's output of around 1 mm BOPD is a far cry from that level. There is likely no quick fix. RBN notes in a blog post that it will likely take tens to hundreds of billions in capital investment and possibly a decade to restore Venezuela's production to former levels.

All of this spells opportunity for the companies in the DRLL portfolio, many of whom have previously operated in the country. Should they be induced to return, it would probably be on a very profitable basis. Quoting the RBN post-

With today’s oil industry focused on capital discipline and shareholder returns, it’s hard to see many international companies committing that kind of money without very strong guarantees. The bottom line is it will likely take years for real change, and there is still much chaos and uncertainty.

One aspect that could result in a quicker uptick in Venezuelan production is the introduction of wide-scale fracking. According to a paper presented at the 2023 Unconventional Resources Technology Conference-URTEQ, the La Luna reservoir could be very prospective for frac-stimulation. That represents the same sort of step-change that could rapidly accelerate daily output, in a similar fashion to how it did in the U.S.

Risks

An investment in DRLL is subject to the same risks of oil price fluctuations that impact the companies that comprise DRLL's portfolio. Some of this inherent risk is tempered by the strength of the companies in which DRLL invests, but the fund's modest 2.99% yield may not offset a "dead money" risk. Yield seekers should probably look elsewhere for investments that fit a dividend income profile.

Your takeaway

I am not historically a big fan of ETFs, as I think better returns are available through stock picking by virtue of rigorous analysis. Speaking directly to the opportunity set that DRLL provides, I don't find it compelling. The high expense ratio is several times that of competing energy oriented funds. As an example, the Vanguard Energy Index Fund, (VDE) has a structure very similar to DRLL, and has a much lower expense ratio and a slightly higher yield.

Accordingly, I rate DRLL as a hold. If you're in it, I don't see a risk of loss outside what I've noted above. That said, I certainly don't see a catalyst for it to outperform and provide much in the way of capital appreciation that would make a strong case for owning it. That's not to say that in a bull case for oil where the DRLL constituents rally, DRLL won't tag along. But I think it's the best it can do, and with the asymmetric opportunity that a repricing of oil would bring, I think there are better opportunities in today's market.

data.title: Athabasca Oil: The Peak Price May Be Near
data.description:
Athabasca Oil (ATHOF) is primarily a thermal oil producer. But it is a small one. Generally, the smaller players have higher costs than the larger ones like Cenovus Energy (CVE). For the time being, the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline has increased thermal profits by keeping the discount from light oil historically low. How long that will last is anyone's guess. Just about all producers I follow, including this one, have begun production expansion projects that in the long run will bring back the discount to historical levels. Furthermore, the political uncertainty marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs is threatening a North American recession. So, this may be the time to sell this one and head towards a larger producer like Cenovus Energy to ride out whatever will happen.

Finances

Furthermore, the financial strategy is a bit on the risky side. There is enough political uncertainty that an investor should demand stellar finances. This is especially true for small companies.

(Note: Athabasca Oil is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Management has decided that the long-term debt shown above is evidently good enough because the company now returns 100% of its cash flow to shareholders (one way or another).

But thermal production is a discounted product compared to light oil. Any recession or worse could widen that discount to effectively eliminate the cash flow generated by production.

That is an especially potent threat because this production is not considered particularly low cost. The cost of production is decent enough to be competitive. But that means that one has to think about the bottom of the oil and gas cycle (and how exactly this company makes it to the next recovery).

It would be far better for investors, and less risky, to repay all of that debt and then build the cash balance a whole lot in preparation for what may come next.

Earnings

The earnings at this point are darn good. Thermal earnings as a whole have not been this good in some time.

As shown above, the excellent cash flow would actually allow for a quick paydown. This company's thermal production has relatively long-lived wells with low decline rates. Additionally, any thermal project has a large upfront cost that generates a depreciation component to protect the cash flow (for a very long time). Therefore, the thermal business will cash flow decently even when the company loses money.

But as noted before, the cash flow issue comes from the discount price for light oil declining to widen that discount. The operating netback right now can easily handle the debt balance. But there is no assurance that will continue if oil prices continue to weaken. Historically, this company has had extremely volatile results. That is what makes this unsuitable to hold during all of this uncertainty.

As can be seen from the results above, management has retired a fair amount of stock (lately) rather than repaying the debt. But that makes this management unafraid of financial risk. That is a very big deal when evaluating a management and hence the investment idea.

Stock Price

I wrote for the last article that the peak stock price may be near. Generally, investors often have a lot of time to get out while making a decent profit. That seems to be the case now.

Clearly the stock price has done rather well over the last few years. But for me, the current political uncertainty would have me evaluating exit opportunities at the current time.

My own belief is that the stock is getting to the point where it is no longer responding to good news. This seems to be happening with a fair number of companies I cover. If it keeps up, we will have that periodic cyclical downturn that occurs with this industry even though the industry never really came back into favor. But that is just the way it is sometimes.

Buy Cenovus Energy

When uncertainty abounds as it does now, it is best to move to larger companies until that uncertainty fades.

Cenovus Energy is a company that I have long followed. The debt is investment grade, and the company has some refining to provide some vertical integration. This places the company in a better position than is the case here to survive a cyclical downturn.

Additionally, the company is far larger and therefore has a bit more bargaining power for its pricing when selling its production. You need every little advantage you can get during a cyclical downturn. I also happen to believe that Cenovus is currently undervalued. I am not sure that is the case here given the appreciation of the stock price from very low levels.

Summary

Athabasca Oil is a company that has some darn good results right now. But the stock price would appear to be ignoring those results. That "ignoring" seems to be spreading and is a result that is beyond the control of management. It may be a sign that a cyclical top is either approaching or has passed. Oftentimes, that is the first clue investors get that it is time to end the investment unless they want to hold through the downcycle.

The current financial situation and business environment (in terms of selling prices) look pretty good. But the market tends to look ahead. Right now, the market does not like what it sees.

Therefore, this stock is likely a strong sell at this point. If one wants to maintain a presence in the thermal business, then a conditional buy would be Cenovus Energy.

One thing I find with professional investors that consistently make money is that they buy when their criteria are met and they sell when those criteria are met. They do not care if the stock goes down after they buy unless the story changes materially (and they do have an exit strategy if things go wrong). Nor do they care if the stock continues to appreciate after they sell.

Instead, professional investors have found a way to make money consistently using whatever strategy they developed, and they are constantly improving that strategy.

On the website, I often get questions about the stock going down after a purchase or the stock going up after a sale. But this indicates that the initial research was not comprehensive enough because a stock price movement is causing doubt about buying and selling decisions.

Yet the professionals make money even in risky situations by being gone before a bankruptcy or severe downturn. They make varying amounts of money on an investment that many on the website hold too long (and then write to me about what could have been).

While the situation continues, I often get comments about being able to make more money (because). But when the downturn happens, the people that cashed in their profits, even if way too early, are way ahead of those that held too long and saw their profits disappear. This is where an investing strategy (and sticking to it) is so important. Making money consistently is more important than getting every last penny in each situation.

Risks

The current political uncertainty could be replaced by something constructive and permanent (like a written trade treaty between Canada and the United States). This may allow the current rally to continue.

However, future commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company. Therefore, even if something constructive and permanent happened between Canada and the United States, it still may not be worth the risk to continue to hold the stock.

Athabasca is a competitive producer. But it is not low cost. However, there may be some add-on projects that could lower the cost of its production in the future to change its competitive position.
